posts.name: M. Dildine
posts.cooked:
Given premox and (IMO) a step change improvement in domestic Chardonnay, this could possibly get interesting.

My favorite Chardonnay is from the SCM, specifically from Rhys (Horseshoe is my fav) and Mount Eden Estate. Trout Gulch (Arnot-Roberts, Ceritas and Kutch) also very special. Ridge Monte Bello …

Absolutely world-class, IMO.

I would say that the best value in higher end Chardonnay I have had is Taittinger CdC.

posts.name: P@u1_M3nk3s
posts.cooked: Best I’ve had is a Raveneau Clos. Never had a Montrachet though. Always been a bit too pricey for me. I’ve had some nice SCMs, but nothing as brilliant as Raveneau Clos.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked: The trick question, but correct, answer to this is Champagne. I do think Cali is catching up to Burgundy a lot faster on Chardonnay than Pinot, though. If price is a consideration, I think you can absolutely make a case for California that you probably can’t for Pinot.

posts.name: Scott Tallman
posts.cooked:
As this is subjective and acknowledging the decision early on in my wine journey to not chase the Burgundy dragon due to premox (White) and price/availability (red & white), I’ll go with Oregon.

A few years ago, I would chalk this up to being an Oregon wine homer. But as I’ve tasted more white Burgundy and CA examples (albeit not the top, top end of the pyramid, but producers and wines discussed and beloved on this board), often alongside top OR examples, I’ve yet to experience any CA Chard and very few Burgundies that I liked better than top OR Chards based on quality. When you factor in price/availability/premox and what I consider small differences in quality, I’m happy to remain solely in the OR Chardonnay pool.

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked: The argument that White Burgundy is subject to premox and too expensive and therefore the world’s finest Chardonnay comes from somewhere else is flawed…

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Of course it depends on whether you prefer ageworthy Chardonnay.

As board members will know, Mike Dildine is singing my song. The Santa Cruz Mountains may be the best terroir today for ageworthy Chardonnay, although I am a very long way from knowing where they all are. I understand Australia has a few candidates, especially from the west. His list is impeccable, but I would add Thomas Fogarty and Storrs. There are probably a number of others I don’t know well enough.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:

Champagne and U.S. Chardonnay are completely different wines from White Burgundy. Heck, Chablis is a completely different wine from Puligny or Meursault.

I would vote for d’Auvenay Chevalier-Montrachet, Ramonet Grands Crus from 70s and 80s, Leflaive Chevalier-Montrachet from the 70s and early 1980s (especially 79 and 82); Coche Perrières & Corton-Charlemagne, and Jean-Marie Guffens’ Clos des Petits Croux and La Roche from Pouilly-Fuissé. Between those, it would be very hard to choose in a blind tasting.

From California, some of the best releases from Kalin Cellars and a few impossible things to find such as the 1980 Chalone Reserve would have to be part of the conversation.

I’ve had half a dozen d’Auvenay White Burgundies, and they were superb. Unfortunately, they are now out of my reach both from a price and availability standpoint.

On the other hand, I had a 2017 Walter Scott X-Novo a couple of nights ago that was also superb and much more affordable. Frankly, between Walter Scott, Goodfellow, and a few others, there really isn’t any reason to buy white burgundy any more. I’ve had a number of SCM Chards as well, including Mt Eden, Rhys, Ridge, and others. I think they’re excellent, though for my palate they can, at times, be a little heavier than I prefer.

posts.name: Rick Allen
posts.cooked:
Frankly, between Walter Scott, Goodfellow, and a few others, there really isn’t any reason to buy white burgundy any more.

Unless, of course, you like white burgundy.

Actually I love white burgundy, and through generous friends, I usually have tastes from one or two bottles of relatively top-end white burgundy (premier cru or better) every week. For my cellar, I don’t like the prices, and I don’t like the premox, and I don’t like the drinking five bottles to find one that has the real sap, cut, and complexity that I’m looking for. I’d rather get those qualities more reliably in something affordable. A good number of burgundy houses have figured this out and are making wine here. Perhaps you need to venture out and try something new.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
From Montrachet. More specifically, I would probably punt for Ramonet as others have done - but then I’ve had far fewer top wines from Lafon, DRC, Coche Dury and D’Auvenay.

As an aside, great to see William mention Guffens in the list, and as I have posted on other threads, LEAS deserves a very high ranking too.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Frankly, between Walter Scott, Goodfellow, and a few others, there really isn’t any reason to buy white burgundy any more.

Unless, of course, you like white burgundy.

Actually I love white burgundy, and through generous friends, I usually have tastes from one or two bottles of relatively top-end white burgundy (premier cru or better) every week. For my cellar, I don’t like the prices, and I don’t like the premox, and I don’t like the drinking five bottles to find one that has the real sap, cut, and complexity that I’m looking for. I’d rather get those qualities more reliably in something affordable. A good number of burgundy houses have figured this out and are making wine here. Perhaps you need to venture out and try something new.

You have assumed that I haven’t been to Oregon or had Walter Scott or Goodfellow Chardonnays. I’ve drunk both, and own both. I have several times brought Walter Scott as a ringer to dinners, and served some blind to my tasting group a few months back. This would not be a “new experience for me”. They are nice wines, and the 17 X-Novo could be a ringer for a Meursault village. These wines are not, however, white Burgundy. I do not mean this in a tautological sense, I mean that they do not deliver the same experience that white Burgundy delivers. The only reason to not buy white Burgundy is that you do not want to drink white Burgundy.

If you do not believe current white Burgundy prices are reasonable, that is a personal opinion I cannot argue. However, there is still a really good reason to buy white Burgundy - to drink it.

posts.name: Jon Drummond

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked:
So your favorite, not the world’s finest. Got it, carry on.

Exactly.

If we’re really talking the world’s finest, I think the list is limited to a few grands crus from Chassagne/Puligny and Chablis. I’m priced and premoxed out of those wines, but I used to be able to buy them as special bottles. I’ve had Mount Eden Estate, various Walter Scott and Goodfellow, etc. Those are fine wines, and great values. But I haven’t tasted anything to indicate that they are as fine as Ramonet Le Montrachet or Raveneau Le Clos or Dauvissat Preuses.

Now I don’t think those burgundies are “epiphany” wines that are on a whole other level. I don’t buy into the whole epiphany wine business, either for red burgundy or white burgundy. The quality difference between Goodfellow “Richard’s Cuvee” and any of those wines is one of degree, not of kind.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I looked up Guffens-Heynen but could’t find either the Clos des Petits Croux or La Roche here in Australia. All I could find from the domaine Guffens-Heynen were some Saint-Verans and Mâcon-Pierreclos, of which it seems you rated the Juliettes et Les Vieilles high in 2018 (all are 2018 vintage). I see a La Roche from what I guess is the negoce or the second label Verget. What do you recommend as intro Guffens and is there anything that’s close to the two wines you mentioned?

The Australian importer of Guffens is Robert Walter IIRC, who wrote the excellent book on Champagne, and he might be able to guide you if you got in touch (he might be able to find you something older, too?). But to understand the Guffens-Heynen range, it’s essential to know that in the three sectors where the domaine produces wines—Mâcon-Pierreclos, Saint-Véran, and Pouilly-Fuissé—there are three or more bottlings: beginning with one that uses the later press fractions (which are higher in pH and sugar) and sometimes younger vine or higher yielding fruit from less favored sites; and concluding with a top bottling using first press juice, the lowest yielding vines, and the highest altitude sites. So Pouilly-Fuissé, for example, the range begins with the “C.C.” bottling (in 2018 “Les Trois C”) which is rounder and softer, and culminates with a “Tri des Hauts des Vignes” (in cool vintages, when Guffens makes multiple picks) or a “Premier Jus des Hauts des Vignes” (in hotter vintages, where everything is picked more or less together). (The Clos des Petits Croux exists in a world of its own - it’s a tiny plot that makes one barrel or very occasionally two, and it’s immensely structured and age worthy, but it really needs 10+ years to begin to show all its cards). So, to experience the real Guffens-Heynen fireworks you should look for something with “Tri” or “1er Jus” in the name. I have a few articles in TWA that unpack this all a bit more thoroughly, and perhaps more comprehensibly than this rather hasty exegesis, happy to send you something in DM if you want more detail.

posts.name: J. Cohen
posts.cooked: DRC Montrachet, Leflaive Montrachet, and Leroy d’Auvenay.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Frankly, between Walter Scott, Goodfellow, and a few others, there really isn’t any reason to buy white burgundy any more.

Unless, of course, you like white burgundy.

Actually I love white burgundy, and through generous friends, I usually have tastes from one or two bottles of relatively top-end white burgundy (premier cru or better) every week. For my cellar, I don’t like the prices, and I don’t like the premox, and I don’t like the drinking five bottles to find one that has the real sap, cut, and complexity that I’m looking for. I’d rather get those qualities more reliably in something affordable. A good number of burgundy houses have figured this out and are making wine here. Perhaps you need to venture out and try something new.

If it takes drinking five bottles of white Burgundy to find one you like, you need to be getting better advice on white Burgundy producers to try. Maybe you should get out more.

I’ve had “Tri des Hauts des Vignes” and “Premier Jus des Hauts des Vignes” bottlings before and found them excellent, even outstanding. But I’ve never had one that made me think it was the world’s finest Chardonnay. FWIW, I like this style a lot and feel that various Germans in Rheinhessen are inching along towards making ones as good as G-H.
EDIT: Never seen or had the Clos des Petits Croux, so maybe that one is on another level?

EDIT: Never seen or had the Clos des Petits Croux, so maybe that one is on another level?

How would you characterize the style? I ask because I never had anything like Guffens-Heynen from the Rheinhessen.

But at the end of the day it is both a matter of taste and personal tasting context, too. In that sense, the question is somewhat arid.

I do think that the Guffens wines are, like most top white Burgundy, at their best with at least ten years’ bottle age. But, being from the Mâconnais, they are less likely to be given that much bottle age than the most celebrated appellations of the Côte de Beaune.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I do think that the Guffens wines are, like most top white Burgundy, at their best with at least ten years’ bottle age. But, being from the Mâconnais, they are less likely to be given that much bottle age than the most celebrated appellations of the Côte de Beaune.

In today’s premox era, are you still aging white Burgundies for 10 years or more? I am only talking about wines from 1995 on.

posts.name: P@u1_M3nk3s
posts.cooked:
How would you characterize the style? I ask because I never had anything like Guffens-Heynen from the Rheinhessen.

But at the end of the day it is both a matter of taste and personal tasting context, too. In that sense, the question is somewhat arid.

I do think that the Guffens wines are, like most top white Burgundy, at their best with at least ten years’ bottle age. But, being from the Mâconnais, they are less likely to be given that much bottle age than the most celebrated appellations of the Côte de Beaune.

I find both have a very floral aspect to them. Very appealing. It appears that I haven’t actually written up very many notes on these guys, but found these two:

  • 2006 Guffens-Heynen Mâcon-Pierreclos Le Chavigne Trie des Hauts de Chavigne - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Mâcon-Pierreclos (1/16/2016)
    No notes. One bottle was pop and pour, the other was decanted. Both drunk early and the decanted one finished after a few hours. Both were stellar and fresh. This is my favorite Maconnaise. Outstanding. (94 pts.)
  • 2005 Guffens-Heynen Mâcon-Pierreclos Le Chavigne Tri des 25 ans - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Mâcon-Pierreclos (8/23/2015)
    This wine had a really great showing tonight. The nose was a pleasant mix of almond, vanilla, mineral and flowers. The palate was a nice mix of mineral, citrus, almond and vanilla, lightly chalky. The body of this wine is heavier than a Côte d’Or, being similar to a more fleet-footed California chard minus the tropical exotica. It’s got a bit of mineral zing without the electricity of its compadres from the Côte. Excellent to Outstanding.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
How would you characterize the style? I ask because I never had anything like Guffens-Heynen from the Rheinhessen.

But at the end of the day it is both a matter of taste and personal tasting context, too. In that sense, the question is somewhat arid.

I do think that the Guffens wines are, like most top white Burgundy, at their best with at least ten years’ bottle age. But, being from the Mâconnais, they are less likely to be given that much bottle age than the most celebrated appellations of the Côte de Beaune.

I find both have a very floral aspect to them. Very appealing. It appears that I haven’t actually written up very many notes on these guys, but found these two:

  • 2006 Guffens-Heynen Mâcon-Pierreclos Le Chavigne Trie des Hauts de Chavigne - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Mâcon-Pierreclos (1/16/2016)
    No notes. One bottle was pop and pour, the other was decanted. Both drunk early and the decanted one finished after a few hours. Both were stellar and fresh. This is my favorite Maconnaise. Outstanding. (94 pts.)
  • 2005 Guffens-Heynen Mâcon-Pierreclos Le Chavigne Tri des 25 ans - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Mâcon-Pierreclos (8/23/2015)
    This wine had a really great showing tonight. The nose was a pleasant mix of almond, vanilla, mineral and flowers. The palate was a nice mix of mineral, citrus, almond and vanilla, lightly chalky. The body of this wine is heavier than a Côte d’Or, being similar to a more fleet-footed California chard minus the tropical exotica. It’s got a bit of mineral zing without the electricity of its compadres from the Côte. Excellent to Outstanding.

Those seem like pretty good notes! Our scores for the 2006 are the same. The 2005 you haven’t rated but I think it’s one of the best white Burgundies of the vintage: it was a ripe year, but you’ll find that in the Côte de Beaune, too; and it has huge levels of dry extract. I scored it 95 and can’t wait to try it again in a decade. If you can find a 2005 white Burgundy from the Côte de Beaune with more “mineral zing” that isn’t from domaine d’Auvenay, Coche-Dury or, caveat premox, Leflaive & Pierre Morey, I would love to know what it is!

posts.name: P@u1_M3nk3s
posts.cooked: William, flattery will get you everywhere, at least in my case! That “Excellent to Outstanding” for the unrated bottle means 93 or 94, couldn’t decide. And not sure exactly which Côte de Beaune wine I was referring to then - I vaguely recall an electric Morey as well as some nice Antoine Jobard from that time.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Actually I love white burgundy, and through generous friends, I usually have tastes from one or two bottles of relatively top-end white burgundy (premier cru or better) every week. For my cellar, I don’t like the prices, and I don’t like the premox, and I don’t like the drinking five bottles to find one that has the real sap, cut, and complexity that I’m looking for. I’d rather get those qualities more reliably in something affordable. A good number of burgundy houses have figured this out and are making wine here. Perhaps you need to venture out and try something new.

If it takes drinking five bottles of white Burgundy to find one you like, you need to be getting better advice on white Burgundy producers to try. Maybe you should get out more.

I’ll tell my friends that are still buying and sharing their white Burgundy that they need to do better. And while COVID-19 has put a serious damper on things, it’s amazing how many opportunities to taste there are when you live in wine country.

I find the attitude of “there is no need to buy white Burgundy anymore” far more dismissive than anything proponents of white Burgundy have said in this thread. Chardonnay is, by some distance, my favorite white grape, so I am always interested in finding new sources for it. I own New Zealand Chardonnay, California Chardonnay, Oregon Chardonnay, and I’m always trying new things. I would never claim there’s no reason to buy Oregon Chardonnay.
You turned a cheeky joke into a rant about the supposed close-mindedness of white Burgundy fans, which is entirely in your imagination. Doctor, heal thyself.

As an aside, I do agree with Howard - if it takes you five bottles of primer cru white Burgundy to find one that matches the quality of a good Oregon Chardonnay, your friends have made some extremely unfortunate purchases.

posts.name: Richard Albert
posts.cooked: 86 Ramonet Montrachet proved itself several times.

Both Michel Niellon Batard and Chevalier from the 70’s through 1990. Mind bending wines in 79 83 85 86 88 89 and 90.

The wheels then feel off the truck beginning in 1995 though those 95’s were great on release.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:

More importantly than that, we have a person who studies Oregon wines but the only Burgundies he drinks are what are put in front of him. He admits therefore that he has not made any study of Burgundy, its producers or vineyards (just drinks what he friends put in front of him) but somehow thinks he knows all there is to know about Burgundy. I would call that extreme arrogance.

As an aside, I do agree with Howard - if it takes you five bottles of primer cru white Burgundy to find one that matches the quality of a good Oregon Chardonnay, your friends have made some extremely unfortunate purchases.

My apologies, Greg. I guess my “rant” was a little too subtle for you. People on CA (witness the person who started this thread) extol the virtues of their local wines. The Aussies love Aussie wines and the Kiwis will give a shout-out for their local stuff. The folks on the East Coast have much easier, faster, and cheaper access to Burgundy (For you, flying to Paris takes about an hour longer than it takes me to get to New York - and then I have to get to Europe), so they love Burgundy. Hell, if I lived in New York, I’d probably love Burgundy even more than I already do. We all love whatever is local to where we live, and, generally, we know those wines better than anyone else.

As for my comment about tasting five to find one, that is probably a bit of an exaggeration, though I’ve had enough pre-moxed and advanced 2014s, 2010s and 2007s, that along with the cardinal sin (from my perspective) of slathering on the oak (PYCM and just about any Corton Charlemagne anyone?) the ratio is pretty bad.

Let me ask you a question though. If I was looking for a White Burgundy that was available for less than $100 that has the sap, cut, and complexity that, to you, define White Burgundy, what would you recommend? I’ll find it and try it.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
My apologies, Greg. I guess my “rant” was a little too subtle for you. People on CA (witness the person who started this thread) extol the virtues of their local wines. The Aussies love Aussie wines and the Kiwis will give a shout-out for their local stuff. The folks on the East Coast have much easier, faster, and cheaper access to Burgundy (For you, flying to Paris takes about an hour longer than it takes me to get to New York - and then I have to get to Europe), so they love Burgundy. Hell, if I lived in New York, I’d probably love Burgundy even more than I already do. We all love whatever is local to where we live, and, generally, we know those wines better than anyone else.

First, the idea that I like Burgundy because I live in New York is…just…what? I started my wine journey in California, where I went to school. Living in New York makes absolutely no difference. I’m not touting Long Island Chardonnay because it’s close at hand. I could just as easily be buying Aubert (easier, frankly). California (or Oregon, for that matter) is more local to me than Burgundy!
Second, I have absolutely no objection to anyone extolling their local stuff. I have even said the local stuff is good. What I found dismissive and close minded is your statement that there is no reason to buy white Burgundy.

As for my comment about tasting five to find one, that is probably a bit of an exaggeration, though I’ve had enough pre-moxed and advanced 2014s, 2010s and 2007s, that along with the cardinal sin (from my perspective) of slathering on the oak (PYCM and just about any Corton Charlemagne anyone?) the ratio is pretty bad.

Man tries PYCM, declares all Burgundy oaky. This would be like me trying Ponzi and declaring all Oregon Pinot Noir overoaked and alcoholic.
But this is the crux of the issue; if you’re judging Burgundy by PYCM, you don’t really know white Burgundy very well. PYCM makes Burgundy in a very distinctive style - not many producers combine the oak, the tropical flavors and the reduction. There are plenty of people who can pick out PYCM in blind tastings (this is not a difficult skill). So if you don’t like that style, you don’t like PYCM, not Burgundy, and there are many other producers to choose from! Personally, I don’t really like PYCM and own zero bottles of it.

As for Corton being slathered in oak - some are, I suppose, but far from most.

Let me ask you a question though. If I was looking for a White Burgundy that was available for less than $100 that has the sap, cut, and complexity that, to you, define White Burgundy, what would you recommend? I’ll find it and try it.

I just had a 2013 Pillot Vergers Clos St. Marc this past weekend that was absolutely spectacular that I picked up for less than $100 this year. The current vintage will run you a bit more - it’s not a huge production.

I can age SCM Chardonnay for a decade or more without worrying about premox. Rhys, Mount Eden and Monte Bello, for example, have proven to age beautifully.

Is premox no longer an issue with White Burgundy?

It is, yes. If you’re suggesting that white Burgundy isn’t necessarily the best value proposition in the world of Chardonnay, that’s a perfectly valid opinion.

I don’t think Rhys, Mount Eden or Monte Bello, when aged, produce the same thing that aged Burgundy does, but that is, again, purely opinion. (I’ve been on the Rhys list for years, fwiw and like the Chardonnays more than the Pinots.)

Hard to beat Aubert Lauren.

I was surprised that no one had mentioned Aubert to this point. Many of my top chardonnay experiences have been with Aubert wines over the years. Now I’m seeking out WB more earnestly, particularly with age.

We drank a '14 UV-SL the other night on some wild salmon. It was a damn good bottle; with Aubert I think you need to forget them for a while and pull them out after 5-7.

In my view, if price is no issue, you can’t beat the top burgundy estates such as Coche and Raveneau. That said, I once blinded a group of fellow wine folks who regularly drink burg (some of who are on this board) with an Aubert Lauren '12. The Aubert was showing beautifully…several in the group thought it was a Corton Charlemagne!

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
So your favorite, not the world’s finest. Got it, carry on.

Exactly.

If we’re really talking the world’s finest, I think the list is limited to a few grands crus from Chassagne/Puligny and Chablis. I’m priced and premoxed out of those wines, but I used to be able to buy them as special bottles. I’ve had Mount Eden Estate, various Walter Scott and Goodfellow, etc. Those are fine wines, and great values. But I haven’t tasted anything to indicate that they are as fine as Ramonet Le Montrachet or Raveneau Le Clos or Dauvissat Preuses.

Now I don’t think those burgundies are “epiphany” wines that are on a whole other level. I don’t buy into the whole epiphany wine business, either for red burgundy or white burgundy. The quality difference between Goodfellow “Richard’s Cuvee” and any of those wines is one of degree, not of kind.

Lee,

Out of curiosity how old was the oldest Mount Eden Estate Chardonnay that you’ve had. Not what vintage. How old was it when consumed.

IMO this wine consistently peaks at about 20 years of age. Before then, I agree ‘fine wines and great values’. Once you get to 20 years, IMO you can have great wine. I’ve only had a few vintages of Ramonet Montrachet, none old enough, but half a dozen or more of Dauvissat Preuses, mostly at 10 - 20+ years. I find Mount Eden on the same level. YMWV (your mileage will vary).

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Mel - there are certainly great examples from NZ, but I wouldn’t put any of them into a “world’s best” category. Neudorf Moutere is probably the most consistent and comparable to GC depth, power and complexity. Kumeu has made some excellent wines, perhaps less consistent. Opinions are split about which is their best wine - I would say the Mate’s Vineyard, but others are wrong . Bell Hill is beautifully styled and has been compared to very high end Chablis.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Pretty sure all of these threads should be titled, The World’s best (insert variety) is from France what say you?

The world’s best rieslings are from Germany.

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked:
So your favorite, not the world’s finest. Got it, carry on.

Exactly.

If we’re really talking the world’s finest, I think the list is limited to a few grands crus from Chassagne/Puligny and Chablis. I’m priced and premoxed out of those wines, but I used to be able to buy them as special bottles. I’ve had Mount Eden Estate, various Walter Scott and Goodfellow, etc. Those are fine wines, and great values. But I haven’t tasted anything to indicate that they are as fine as Ramonet Le Montrachet or Raveneau Le Clos or Dauvissat Preuses.

Now I don’t think those burgundies are “epiphany” wines that are on a whole other level. I don’t buy into the whole epiphany wine business, either for red burgundy or white burgundy. The quality difference between Goodfellow “Richard’s Cuvee” and any of those wines is one of degree, not of kind.

Lee,

Out of curiosity how old was the oldest Mount Eden Estate Chardonnay that you’ve had. Not what vintage. How old was it when consumed.

IMO this wine consistently peaks at about 20 years of age. Before then, I agree ‘fine wines and great values’. Once you get to 20 years, IMO you can have great wine. I’ve only had a few vintages of Ramonet Montrachet, none old enough, but half a dozen or more of Dauvissat Preuses, mostly at 10 - 20+ years. I find Mount Eden on the same level. YMWV (your mileage will vary).

Dan Kravitz

Dan, I’ve had Mount Eden roughly a handful of times – once young, mostly aged in the 15-25 year range. And they were certainly fine wines, one might even call them great. But IME the average performance was just a notch below that of a Dauvissat grand cru. Not that big a difference, but a noticable one. And the bell curves did have some overlap – certainly, the best bottle of Mt Eden that I’ve had was as better than the worst bottle of Preuses.

I also think that Dauvissat wines require some experience with them to get the best experience from. Someone (Stuart N?) goes on about this, and sounds like a bit of a crank – but he is right.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Lee,

Thanks. Our mileage varies here, which is perfectly normal. You’ve obviously had enough Mount Eden with age to pass your own judgment. I’ve had enough Dauvissat ‘Preuses’ to consider my opinion reasonably well informed. Plus I’ve had hundreds of other Chablis from different producers, vintages and vineyards. We like what we like. I just wish I had more experience with Raveneau at the top level.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked: Dan – yeah, I think we all wish we had more experience with Raveneau at the top level.

posts.name: Roger Smith
posts.cooked: Rombauer from mom’s club cause I don’t pay…

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Pretty sure all of these threads should be titled, The World’s best (insert variety) is from France what say you?

The world’s best rieslings are from Germany.

Not all of them.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Ceritas Zephyr. Drops mic, exits.

There can be only one.

I appreciate your enthusiasm…we all have our favorites.

But I would doubt that any producer is simply the best every vintage.

I love both Raveneau and Dauvisaat, but had whiffs from both too. Many more whiffs and pre-mox from my favorite Burgundy producers. I’m guessing that you’ll see the same with Ceritas too(perhaps bot pre-mox), if you continue to drink them regularly for a couple of decades. I make pretty consistently good Chardonnay every year, but there are still ups and downs. The longer I do this, the more I learn and experience, the more the level of quality stays higher and the higher the potential is. But the greatest basketball players don’t make every shot.

That said, while white Burgundy is still probably the finest Chardonnay, given that the Willamette Valley is 50 years old, and they have had 1000s of years, the wines currently being made in the Valley speak to a future potential that may be as high as the finest current white Burgundy.

Most of all, I really appreciate that my wines are seen as of a quality to be mentioned in comparison with the greatest producers in the world.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Unless, of course, you like white burgundy.

Actually I love white burgundy, and through generous friends, I usually have tastes from one or two bottles of relatively top-end white burgundy (premier cru or better) every week. For my cellar, I don’t like the prices, and I don’t like the premox, and I don’t like the drinking five bottles to find one that has the real sap, cut, and complexity that I’m looking for. I’d rather get those qualities more reliably in something affordable. A good number of burgundy houses have figured this out and are making wine here. Perhaps you need to venture out and try something new.

If it takes drinking five bottles of white Burgundy to find one you like, you need to be getting better advice on white Burgundy producers to try. Maybe you should get out more.

While I might once have agreed with this, over the past few years I feel my experiences are changing. Especially if sap, cut, and energy/tension are necessary to the experience.

I still really like a lot of white Burgundy producers: Carillon, Mikulski, Jobard, Ente, Coche, Raveneau, Dauvissat, Moreau, Lafon, etc. but I don’t find the cut and minerality that I used to, other than Dauvissat. When we’ve done side by side tastings with Oregon chardonnay and white Burgundy, I am routinely surprised by how big the white Burgundies present.

Lately, big has also included ripe. And far too often, tired at ages the wines should be strong. A 2012 Paul Pernod P-M 1er Cru that was $80 on release was butterscotchy and less than good, much less stellar. 2016 Louis Michel MdT is a nice wine, but not the saline, linear, edgy wine that I feel Louis Michel wines were in the 90s. And while still a bargain at $40, I regret buying 4 bottles instead of one.



Honestly, I stated that I felt that the finest Chardonnay in the world is still probably Burgundy, but after a moments reflection, I would change my vote to Champagne.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: It used to be that the miss on Burgundy vintages were wines that were underripe. Now it is wines that are overripe. So, in recent vintages, I have bought from vintages with better acidity like 2014, 2016 and 2017 and bought more sparingly or not at all from vintages like 2015 and 2018. I have followed a similar pattern for German wines (with different vintages) for the same reason.

The best two I have had so far would have to be:

2014 Domaine de la Romanée-Conti Montrachet Montrachet Grand Cru
2004 Domaine d’Auvenay (Lalou Bize-Leroy) Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Folatières

posts.name: M. Dildine
posts.cooked: Tasted an '18 Rhys “Horseshoe” SCM last night. One of the best young Chardonnays I’ve ever tasted.

posts.name: Per Svenonius
posts.cooked:
I’m now looking at the 2022 Guffens-Heynen primeur offer and so far there seems to be no Pouilly-Fuissé 1er Jus des Premiers Crus.

Last and only release of this label was for 2020 and I then assumed with the new 1er cru status of some Puilly-Fuissé that this was the new equivalent of the Priemiers jus des Hauts des Vinges. There is however a primeur release of Priemiers jus des Hauts des Vinges in 2022. It would be very helpful to better understand the diffrences if any or small between the two designations.

I have also tried to get as much info as possible about how successful in general Pouilly-Fuissé was in 2022. Yes we know 2022 was a very hot and ripe year in France in general but for Macon and Pouilly Fuissé there is not much written yet. I found one food site (I have no idea how serious they are) that rated 2022 Pouilly-Fuissé 5 of 5 (the “consensus great” 2020 was rated 4 of 5) but it was only a chart and no other information was given.

posts.name: Cameron Clark
posts.cooked:
Not read the whole thread, so sorry if this is a repeat, but maybe the correct title of this thread is ‘The Worlds finest Chardonnay Came From….’

And my answer to that would be a fine white Burg from a great vintage before promox started and things got warmer. I’ve had a few vintages of Ramonet Montrachet, but the wine in remember most is actually the 91 Batard. Not as much fruit as modern Chardonnay, but so much minerality.

I’m sure as vineyards heat up, great wine will still be made all over and areas once thought to be too cold, will maybe end up producing examples that are even better than the wines I remember. But that’s all guess work.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It really changes from year to year, with Guffens following the same general principles but adapting to the vintage. Premiers Jus des Hauts des Vignes 2022 includes the Croux, which wasn’t classified as premier cru because it’s not south-facing (I suspect as much as anything because the INAO didn’t want to set any precedents), as well as Guffens’ last harvest of La Roche. Reviews will be coming out before too long, and as ever Guffens’ viticulture and pressing approach has delivered wines with full ripeness but very low pH. For him, 2022 isn’t quite on the level of 2020, but it’s a very good vintage.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
The World’s Finest Egri Bikaver comes from Eger.
The World’s Finest Chablis comes from Chablis.
The World’s Finest Margaret River Chardonnay comes from the Margaret River region.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
FWIW,
We tasted some Pierre Morey Batard this weekend–2011 and 2005. (An 1988 unfortunately was corked.) To my surprise, actually, not all fruit forward, no tropicals, but incredible depth and minerality. Both were too young and perhaps should have been decanted but the '11 opened nicely in the glass over time. Of course, no one would expect '11 to be hugely fruited, but neither was the '05, which really was a coiled monster.

posts.name: Matt Kelso
posts.cooked: Best vineyards for me are Corton-Charlemagne and Chevalier Montrachet. That said, old California chards from the early- to mid-1970s blow everyone away at that age bracket (Monterey County, Napa, Sonoma)
